Client 2: And I’m Susan. Same, but I’m more focused on month-to-month—like, what changes in our routine if we do X versus Y.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill in. William, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, and then we’ll pin it down?
Client 1: All-in, it’s 134400 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And Susan, on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in—mortgage, groceries, kids, everything?
Client 2: It’s heavy. Total monthly expenses are 10650 a month.

Client 1: All-in, it’s 134400 a year.

Client 2: It’s heavy. Total monthly expenses are 10650 a month.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just writing that down. Within that, I want to separate out the debt payments so we can see the fixed commitments. Between the mortgage and any other loans, what are you paying per month total toward debts?
Client 2: I mean the mortgage is the big one—like 4,350—and then there’s that other loan at 600, so monthly debt payments total 4950.

Client 2: It’s heavy. Total monthly expenses are 10650 a month.

Client 2: I mean the mortgage is the big one—like 4,350—and then there’s that other loan at 600, so monthly debt payments total 4950.

Client 1: Total investable assets are 702150.

Advisor: Great. We’ll unpack what makes up that 702150 in a minute—retirement accounts, taxable, cash, anything like that. Before we do, just to make sure I’m not missing anything big: income is 134400 annually, expenses 10650 monthly, and debt payments 4950 monthly—sound right?
Client 1: Yep, that’s right.

Client 1: The mortgage balance outstanding is 133150.
Advisor: Got it. And do you remember the home’s current value you’re using—like what you’d put as the property value today?
Client 2: We’ve been using 345400 as the property value.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, Susan. Now the other loan you mentioned at 600 a month—what’s the balance on that one right now?
Client 2: The non-mortgage debt outstanding is 10100.

Client 2: The non-mortgage debt outstanding is 10100.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m not double-counting anything—between the mortgage and that other loan, what do you consider your total loans outstanding right now?
Client 1: Total loans outstanding is 143250.
Advisor: Great. While we’re on the mortgage for a second—do you happen to know the rate and when it’s scheduled to be paid off?
Client 1: Yeah, it’s 6.48% and the final payment date is 2029-09-12.

Client 2: And I’m Susan. Same, but I’m more focused on month-to-month—like, what changes in our routine if we do X versus Y.

Advisor: Thanks. That helps me frame the cash-flow pressure you mentioned.
Advisor: Last one in this section—taxes. Do you know what bracket you typically land in, roughly? Even a ballpark is fine.
Client 1: I think it’s around the mid-20s—hang on—our tax bracket band is 24%.
Advisor: Perfect, 24%. I’ll stick with that for planning assumptions.
Advisor: And just so I’m not making assumptions—Susan, when you say five dependents, that’s the kids you two are supporting day-to-day?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Susan—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement path, but also not feeling like one surprise blows us up.
Client 2: And I’m Susan. Same, but I’m more focused on month-to-month—like, what changes in our routine if we do X versus Y.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill in. William, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, and then we’ll pin it down?
Client 1: All-in, it’s 134400 a year.

Client 1: All-in, it’s 134400 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And Susan, on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run, all-in—mortgage, groceries, kids, everything?

Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just writing that down. Within that, I want to separate out the debt payments so we can see the fixed commitments. Between the mortgage and any other loans, what are you paying per month total toward debts?
Client 2: I mean the mortgage is the big one—like 4,350—and then there’s that other loan at 600, so monthly debt payments total 4950.

Client 1: Yeah, that’s the picture.
Advisor: Totally hear you, Susan—there are a lot of pieces, but we’ll keep it simple. I want to zoom out to the household basics for a minute.
Advisor: First, just to confirm—William and Susan, are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And household size—just the two of you as adults in the home, right?

Advisor: PA—got it. And country-wise, you’re in the US, right?
Client 1: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is more about how we work together—are you engaging me through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, or is this through a bank or insurance agent setup?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, thanks. And then risk—when markets get choppy, how would you describe your comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?

Advisor: Perfect. Next one is more about how we work together—are you engaging me through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, or is this through a bank or insurance agent setup?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, thanks. And then risk—when markets get choppy, how would you describe your comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. Like, I don’t want big swings, but we know we can’t be all cash.
Advisor: Yep, that makes sense. And just to connect it back—earlier you mentioned the cash-flow pressure with the mortgage and everything. Are you both still comfortable calling it moderate overall, not conservative?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Susan—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement path, but also not feeling like one surprise blows us up.
Client 2: And I’m Susan. Same, but I’m more focused on month-to-month—like, what changes in our routine if we do X versus Y.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill in. William, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, and then we’ll pin it down?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Susan—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m William. Honestly, I want a clear plan—retirement path, but also not feeling like one surprise blows us up.
Client 2: And I’m Susan. Same, but I’m more focused on month-to-month—like, what changes in our routine if we do X versus Y.

Advisor: Okay. Switching gears to income for a minute—William, earlier you gave me the household gross of 134400. How does that break down for you versus Susan?
Client 1: My gross income annual is 104350.
Client 2: And mine is 30000 a year, but I’m not working right now—so it’s kind of… not from a job.
Advisor: Right—thank you. And William, do you have multiple income streams on your side—like business income pieces that come in different ways?
Client 1: Yes—business income annual 42450, and another business income annual 11000, and then business income ad hoc 80950.

Advisor: Perfect. That gives me enough to start sketching options that match a moderate risk approach and your monthly reality in PA.
Advisor: Alright. Before I build the first cut of recommendations, I want to make sure I’ve got Susan’s side of the income and work situation right—because that affects taxes, cash flow, and insurance needs.
Advisor: Susan, in the household, you’re William’s spouse/partner, correct?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm how you want me to label you two in my notes—William is Client 1 and Susan you’re Client 2, right?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Susan—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting feel useful for you?

Client 2: There is, yeah. It’s basically 30000 gross for the year.
